   283→### 5. The "Generic Expert" Anti-Pattern
   284→
   285→**Claude might generate:**
   286→```markdown
   287→# Fortune 50 Marketing Expert
   288→
   289→You are a world-class CMO with decades of experience leading marketing at Fortune 50 companies. You understand business strategy, brand building, and customer acquisition.
   290→
   291→When asked about marketing, provide comprehensive, executive-level guidance...
   292→```
   293→
   294→**Why this fails:**
   295→- **Persona theater**: "You are a CMO" doesn't change Claude's knowledge
   296→- **No differentiation**: Generic "expert" framing vs. specific capabilities
   297→- **Missing domain vocabulary**: Doesn't use actual marketing terminology
   298→- **No decision frameworks**: Explains role, not how to do the work
   299→
   300→**Instead use:**
   301→```markdown
   302→# Fortune 50 Marketing Expert
   303→
   304→## When to Activate
   305→
   306→Use for marketing strategy decisions that involve:
   307→- GTM planning, positioning, messaging architecture
   308→- Brand strategy, portfolio management, master brand vs. sub-brands
   309→- Campaign planning with media mix modeling
   310→- Customer segmentation beyond basic demographics
   311→- Marketing analytics (CAC, LTV, attribution modeling)
   312→
   313→## Core Frameworks
   314→
   315→When asked about marketing strategy, default to:
   316→
   317→1. **GTM Decision Tree**
   318→   - Product-led vs. sales-led motion?
   319→   - If revenue <$100K ACV → product-led (PLG)
   320→   - If revenue >$100K ACV → sales-led (SLG)
   321→   - Reasoning: Enterprise sales cost of $50K+ per deal
   322→
   323→2. **Positioning Framework (April Dunford)**
   324→   - Best customers: Who gets 10x value?
   325→   - Competitive alternatives: What do they use today?
   326→   - Unique capabilities: What can only you do?
   327→   - Value theorem: Why does it matter?
   328→   - Category: Where do you win?
   329→
   330→3. **Message Hierarchy**
   331→   ```
   332→   Master Brand Promise (emotional)
   333→   ↓
   334→   Value Proposition (functional benefit)
   335→   ↓
   336→   Proof Points (evidence/features)
   337→   ↓
   338→   Calls to Action
   339→   ```
   340→
   341→## Domain Vocabulary
   342→
   343→Use actual marketing terminology:
   344→- **Attribution modeling** (not "tracking conversions")
   345→- **Media mix optimization** (not "choosing channels")
   346→- **Demand waterfall** (not "funnel stages")
   347→- **CAC payback period** (not "return on marketing spend")
   348→- **Share of voice** (not "brand awareness")
   349→
   350→## Red Flags to Watch For
   351→
   352→When user asks for marketing help, check for:
   353→- ❌ "We need to do social media" → Probe: Why? What's the business objective?
   354→- ❌ "Our brand needs to be modern" → Define: Modern to whom? What behavior change?
   355→- ❌ "We need more leads" → Quality or quantity? What's current conversion rate?
   356→
   357→## References
   358→
   359→Detailed playbooks in:
   360→- `references/brand-strategy.md` - Portfolio architecture, brand equity
   361→- `references/campaign-playbook.md` - Media mix, campaign execution
   362→- `references/positioning-frameworks.md` - Dunford, Christensen JTBD
   363→```
   364→
   365→**Why this works:**
   366→- **Activation clarity**: Specific scenarios when skill is needed
   367→- **Named frameworks**: Uses real methodologies (Dunford, JTBD)
   368→- **Domain expertise**: Actual terminology professionals use
   369→- **Decision support**: Guides which framework to apply when
   370→- **Critical thinking**: Red flags prevent bad requests
   371→
   372→**Rule:** Domain skills must demonstrate expertise through frameworks, vocabulary, and decision-making—not just claim expertise.
   373→
   374→**Sources:**
   375→- [Claude Docs - Skill Authoring Best Practices](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices)
   376→- [Optimizely - Writing AI Instructions](https://www.optimizely.com/insights/blog/how-to-write-ai-instructions/)
   377→
   378→---
   379→
   380→## Common Mistakes
   381→
   382→### Mistake 1: Ambiguous or Buried Instructions
   383→
   384→**What happens:** Critical instructions get lost in long files or conflicting requirements
   385→**Why it's wrong:** LLMs struggle with buried directives—like telling someone to go left while pointing right
   386→**Prevalence:** Extremely common in first-draft skills
   387→
   388→**Example:**
   389→```markdown
   390→# Code Review Skill
   391→
   392→Review code for quality, security, and performance. Check for bugs and suggest improvements. Make sure the code follows best practices and is well-documented. Look for security issues and performance problems. Ensure accessibility standards are met. By the way, ALWAYS start with security checks first, and never approve code with type safety issues.
   393→```
   394→
   395→**Correct approach:**
   396→```markdown
   397→# Code Review Skill
   398→
   399→## Priority Order (Follow This Sequence)
   400→
   401→1. **Security & Type Safety** (BLOCKING)
   402→   - Check first, before anything else
   403→   - Any issues here = immediate stop, do not continue
   404→
   405→2. **Functionality & Logic** (BLOCKING)
   406→   - Only check if security passes
   407→
   408→3. **Performance & Optimization** (ADVISORY)
   409→   - Only mention if severe issues found
   410→
   411→## Instructions
   412→[Clear, sequential steps...]
   413→```
   414→
   415→**Fix:** Use visual hierarchy, delimiters (### CRITICAL ###), and explicit sequencing. Place critical instructions at start or end, never buried mid-text.
   416→
   417→**Source:** [HackerNoon - Why Prompts Don't Work](https://hackernoon.com/help-my-prompt-is-not-working)
   418→
   419→---
   420→
   421→### Mistake 2: Missing Few-Shot Examples
   422→
   423→**What happens:** Skill explains what to do but Claude doesn't understand the expected pattern
   424→**Why it's wrong:** LLMs learn format and reasoning style from examples, not just instructions
   425→**When it matters most:** Complex output formats, specific reasoning patterns, domain-specific analysis
   426→
   427→**Example - Missing Examples:**
   428→```markdown
   429→# Technical Debt Analysis
   430→
   431→Analyze the codebase for technical debt and create a prioritized report with severity levels.
   432→
   433→Categories: Architecture, Code Quality, Dependencies, Documentation, Testing
   434→Severity: Critical, High, Medium, Low
   435→```
   436→
   437→**Correct Approach - With Examples:**
   438→```markdown
   439→# Technical Debt Analysis
   440→
   441→## Output Format
   442→
   443→For each issue found, use this structure:
   444→
   445→**Example 1:**
   446→```
   447→### [CRITICAL] Hardcoded Database Credentials
   448→**Category:** Security / Architecture
   449→**Location:** `src/db/connection.ts:15-18`
   450→**Impact:** Production data exposure risk, blocks SOC2 compliance
   451→**Effort:** 2 hours (move to environment variables)
   452→**Fix Priority:** P0 - Fix before next deploy
   453→
   454→**Rationale:** Committed credentials = immediate security vulnerability. Any dev with repo access has production DB access.
   455→```
   456→
   457→**Example 2:**
   458→```
   459→### [HIGH] No Input Validation on User Endpoints
   460→**Category:** Security / Code Quality
   461→**Location:** `src/api/user/*.ts` (12 endpoints)
   462→**Impact:** SQL injection, XSS vulnerabilities
   463→**Effort:** 1 week (add Zod schemas + validation middleware)
   464→**Fix Priority:** P1 - Sprint 1
   465→
   466→**Rationale:** User-facing endpoints accept raw input. Found 3 instances of string interpolation in queries (lines 45, 78, 103 in user-service.ts).
   467→```
   468→
   469→**Example 3:**
   470→```
   471→### [MEDIUM] Outdated Dependencies (React 16)
   472→**Category:** Dependencies / Maintenance
   473→**Location:** `package.json`
   474→**Impact:** Missing performance improvements, no hooks, security patches unavailable
   475→**Effort:** 2-3 weeks (breaking changes in upgrade path)
   476→**Fix Priority:** P2 - Plan for Q2
   477→
   478→**Rationale:** React 16 EOL was 2021. Not urgent but blocks future library updates and team productivity.
   479→```
   480→
   481→## When Analyzing Real Code
   482→
   483→Follow the pattern above:
   484→1. Lead with severity in heading: [CRITICAL], [HIGH], [MEDIUM], [LOW]
   485→2. Title describes the problem, not the symptom
   486→3. Location = exact file paths and line numbers
   487→4. Impact = business/user consequence, not technical description
   488→5. Effort = realistic time estimate
   489→6. Rationale = why this matters, evidence found
   490→
   491→Never just list issues—explain priority logic.
   492→```
   493→
   494→**Why examples matter:**
   495→- Shows exact format expected (markdown structure, headings)
   496→- Demonstrates reasoning pattern (impact → effort → priority)
   497→- Clarifies tone and specificity level
   498→- Provides severity calibration (what's "critical" vs "medium")
   499→
   500→**Research Finding:** Few-shot learning allows AI to recognize patterns and apply them to new situations. For complicated tasks, 2-3 carefully selected examples are critical.
   501→
   502→**Source:** [Medium - 5 Prompt Engineering Mistakes](https://medium.com/@kaushalsinh73/5-prompt-engineering-mistakes-that-cost-me-accuracy-and-how-to-fix-them-83204a638769)
   503→
   504→---
   505→
   506→### Mistake 3: No Verification or Self-Critique
   507→
   508→**What happens:** Skill produces output without quality checks, leading to hallucinations and errors
   509→**Why it's wrong:** LLMs make mistakes but can catch them if prompted to self-critique
   510→**Evidence:** Research shows prompting LLM to critique its own work before final answer significantly reduces errors
   511→
   512→**Example - No Verification:**
   513→```markdown
   514→# SQL Query Generator
   515→
   516→Generate optimized SQL queries based on user requirements.
   517→
   518→## Steps
   519→1. Understand the data model
   520→2. Write the SQL query
   521→3. Return the query to user
   522→```
   523→
   524→**Correct Approach - With Verification:**
   525→```markdown
   526→# SQL Query Generator
   527→
   528→Generate optimized SQL queries based on user requirements.
   529→
   530→## Process
   531→
   532→### Step 1: Understand Requirements
   533→- What tables are involved?
   534→- What's the expected output?
   535→- Any performance constraints?
   536→
   537→### Step 2: Draft Query
   538→Write initial SQL query.
   539→
   540→### Step 3: Self-Critique (REQUIRED)
   541→
   542→Before returning query, check:
   543→
   544→**Correctness:**
   545→- [ ] Does this actually answer the user's question?
   546→- [ ] Are JOIN conditions correct?
   547→- [ ] Is WHERE clause logic sound?
   548→
   549→**Performance:**
   550→- [ ] Are indexes being used? (check EXPLAIN)
   551→- [ ] Any N+1 query patterns?
   552→- [ ] Could this be rewritten to avoid subqueries?
   553→
   554→**Safety:**
   555→- [ ] No SQL injection vectors?
   556→- [ ] Parameterized inputs only?
   557→- [ ] Appropriate LIMIT clauses?
   558→
   559→### Step 4: Revise if Needed
   560→
   561→If any critique flags issues, revise query and re-check.
   562→
   563→### Step 5: Present with Explanation
   564→
   565→**SQL Query:**
   566→```sql
   567→[Query here]
   568→```
   569→
   570→**Why this works:**
   571→- Uses index on `user_id` column (fast lookup)
   572→- JOIN on primary keys (optimized)
   573→- LIMIT prevents runaway results
   574→- Parameterized @userId prevents injection
   575→
   576→**Expected Performance:** <100ms for tables <1M rows
   577→```
   578→
   579→**Why verification matters:**
   580→- **Reduces hallucinations**: Forces Claude to double-check logic
   581→- **Catches edge cases**: Self-critique reveals overlooked scenarios
   582→- **Builds trust**: User sees reasoning, not just output
   583→- **Improves accuracy**: Research shows significant error reduction
   584→
   585→**Critical Rule:** For any skill that generates technical output (code, SQL, config, formulas), include mandatory self-critique step.
   586→
   587→**Sources:**
   588→- [Medium - Prompt Engineering Mistakes](https://medium.com/@kaushalsinh73/5-prompt-engineering-mistakes-that-cost-me-accuracy-and-how-to-fix-them-83204a638769)
   589→- [Atlas - Why AI Prompts Fail](https://www.heyatlas.com/blog/ai-prompts-fail-how-to-fix)
   590→
   591→---
   592→
   593→### Mistake 4: Underspecification (Forcing Claude to Guess)
   594→
   595→**What happens:** Skill leaves critical details unspecified, forcing Claude to make assumptions
   596→**Why it's wrong:** Under-specified prompts are 2x more likely to regress across model updates, with accuracy drops exceeding 20%
   597→**When it happens:** Vague output requirements, ambiguous success criteria, missing context about audience/purpose
   598→
   599→**Example - Underspecified:**
   600→```markdown
   601→# Documentation Writer
   602→
   603→Help create clear documentation for technical projects.
   604→
   605→## Instructions
   606→- Write clear explanations
   607→- Use examples where helpful
   608→- Make it understandable
   609→```
   610→
   611→**Problem:** "Clear" to whom? "Understandable" by what audience? What format? How long?
   612→
   613→**Correct Approach - Fully Specified:**
   614→```markdown
   615→# Documentation Writer
   616→
   617→## Required Context (Ask if Not Provided)
   618→
   619→Before writing, determine:
   620→1. **Audience**: Beginners / Intermediate developers / Experts?
   621→2. **Purpose**: Getting started / API reference / Conceptual guide?
   622→3. **Format**: README / Wiki page / Tutorial / API docs?
   623→4. **Length constraint**: Quick reference (<500 words) / Comprehensive guide (2000+ words)?
   624→
   625→## Writing Standards by Audience
   626→
   627→### For Beginners
   628→- **Assume zero prior knowledge** of the domain
   629→- Define every technical term on first use
   630→- Include "why" explanations, not just "how"
   631→- Use analogies to familiar concepts
   632→- Example code must be copy-pasteable and run without modification
   633→
   634→### For Intermediate Developers
   635→- **Assume basic familiarity** with domain concepts
   636→- Focus on "how" with brief "why" context
   637→- Link to deeper explanations rather than explaining basics
   638→- Show common patterns and best practices
   639→- Code examples can assume working dev environment
   640→
   641→### For Experts
   642→- **Assume deep knowledge** of domain and adjacent areas
   643→- Focus on edge cases, performance, and architecture decisions
   644→- Reference specifications and RFCs
   645→- Highlight tradeoffs and alternatives
   646→- Code examples show advanced patterns
   647→
   648→## Structure by Purpose
   649→
   650→### Getting Started Guide
   651→1. **What is this?** (1-2 sentences)
   652→2. **Installation** (exact commands)
   653→3. **Quick Start** (minimal working example in <5 minutes)
   654→4. **Next Steps** (links to deeper docs)
   655→
   656→### API Reference
   657→1. **Function signature** with types
   658→2. **Parameters** (name, type, description, required/optional)
   659→3. **Returns** (type, description)
   660→4. **Example usage** (realistic, not toy examples)
   661→5. **Edge cases / errors** (what can go wrong)
   662→
   663→### Conceptual Guide
   664→1. **Problem statement** (why does this exist?)
   665→2. **Mental model** (how to think about it)
   666→3. **Key concepts** (3-5 main ideas)
   667→4. **Common patterns** (how people actually use it)
   668→5. **Further reading** (links to related topics)
   669→
   670→## Quality Checklist (Run Before Delivering)
   671→
   672→- [ ] Is the first sentence a clear, jargon-free explanation?
   673→- [ ] Can the reader complete a task after reading this?
   674→- [ ] Are code examples tested and working?
   675→- [ ] Are errors/edge cases documented?
   676→- [ ] Is there a "Next Steps" or "See Also" section?
   677→
   678→## Output Format
   679→
   680→Always include:
   681→
   682→**Target Audience:** [Beginners/Intermediate/Expert]
   683→**Document Type:** [Getting Started/API Reference/Conceptual]
   684→
   685→[Documentation content]
   686→
   687→**Next Steps:**
   688→- [Where to go from here]
   689→```
   690→
   691→**Why full specification matters:**
   692→- **Consistency**: Same inputs produce similar outputs across model versions
   693→- **Reduces rework**: Fewer clarification rounds
   694→- **Prevents regression**: Under-specified prompts are 2x more likely to break with model updates
   695→- **Quality control**: Clear success criteria enable self-critique
   696→
   697→**Research Evidence:** Simply adding more requirements doesn't work (can degrade performance 19%), but specifying the RIGHT requirements—audience, format, constraints—improves reliability.
   698→
   699→**Source:** [ArXiv - Understanding Underspecification in LLM Prompts](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.13360v1)
   700→
   701→---
   702→
   703→### Mistake 5: Description Field as Documentation (Not Discovery)
   704→
   705→**What happens:** Description field contains detailed explanations instead of trigger phrases
   706→**Why it's wrong:** Description is for skill activation, not for explaining what skill does once active
   707→**Impact:** Skill fails to trigger when user uses natural language
   708→
   709→**Example - Wrong:**
   710→```markdown
   711→description: "This skill provides expert-level guidance on technical debt management. It helps engineering teams identify, categorize, and prioritize technical debt across multiple dimensions including code quality, architecture, dependencies, testing, and documentation. Use this skill when conducting code reviews, planning refactoring efforts, or assessing codebase health." (plugin:tech-debt-analyzer@skills)
   712→```
   713→
   714→**Why this fails:**
   715→- User says: "Is this codebase maintainable?"
   716→- Skill doesn't activate because "maintainable" isn't in description
   717→- User says: "We have a lot of code smells"
   718→- Skill doesn't activate because "code smells" isn't in description
   719→
   720→**Correct Approach:**
   721→```markdown
   722→description: "Use when analyzing technical debt, code quality issues, maintainability problems, code smells, architecture issues, dependency problems, missing documentation, security vulnerabilities, testing gaps, refactoring needs, codebase health assessment, or creating technical debt registers." (plugin:tech-debt-analyzer@skills)
   723→```
   724→
   725→**Why this works:**
   726→- **Natural language coverage**: Includes varied phrases users actually say
   727→- **Task-oriented**: "analyzing" "assessing" "creating" = verbs that trigger activation
   728→- **Synonym expansion**: "technical debt" + "code quality issues" + "maintainability problems" cover same concept
   729→- **Concrete outcomes**: "creating technical debt registers" = specific deliverable
   730→
   731→**Rule for Description Fields:**
   732→
   733→**DO:**
   734→- List task verbs: "analyzing", "planning", "creating", "reviewing"
   735→- Include natural language variations: "technical debt" / "code quality" / "maintainability"
   736→- Name specific deliverables: "creating roadmaps", "writing test plans"
   737→- Use third person: "Use when..." or "Help with..."
   738→
   739→**DON'T:**
   740→- Explain what the skill does in detail
   741→- Use qualifier phrases: "expert-level", "comprehensive", "Fortune 50"
   742→- Include methodology explanations
   743→- Write in first person: "I help with..."
   744→
   745→**Testing Activation:** After writing description, ask:
   746→- Would this trigger if user said: "Our code is messy"?
   747→- Would this trigger if user said: "Should we refactor this?"
   748→- Would this trigger if user said: "What needs fixing?"
   749→
   750→If no, add those phrases to description.
   751→
   752→**Source:** [Claude Docs - Skill Authoring Best Practices](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices)
   753→
   754→---
   755→
   756→## Skill-Specific Gotchas
   757→
   758→### Domain Expert Skills (fortune50-*)
   759→
   760→#### Issue 1: Generic Business Advice Instead of Domain Expertise
   761→
   762→**What happens:** Skill provides general business frameworks anyone could find on Google
   763→**Why it's wrong:** "Fortune 50" implies access to insider frameworks, executive decision-making patterns, and real-world tradeoffs
   764→
   765→**Example - Generic:**
   766→```markdown
   767→# Fortune 50 Finance Expert
   768→
   769→## Financial Analysis
   770→
   771→When analyzing financial performance:
   772→- Look at revenue growth
   773→- Check profitability metrics
   774→- Review cash flow
   775→- Compare to industry benchmarks
   776→
   777→Use common metrics like:
   778→- Revenue growth rate
   779→- Gross margin %
   780→- Operating margin %
   781→- EBITDA
   782→- Free cash flow
   783→```
   784→
   785→**This is wrong because:**
   786→- Any business student knows these metrics
   787→- No insight into HOW executives actually use them
   788→- Missing decision frameworks that separate good from great analysis
   789→- Doesn't show tradeoffs or prioritization
   790→
   791→**Expert-Level Approach:**
   792→```markdown
   793→# Fortune 50 Finance Expert
   794→
   795→## Financial Analysis: The CFO Decision Stack
   796→
   797→When a CFO reviews financial performance, they follow this prioritization:
   798→
   799→### Tier 1: Cash Position (Check First, Always)
   800→1. **Days Cash on Hand** = Cash ÷ (Operating Expenses ÷ 365)
   801→   - <30 days = red alert, survival mode
   802→   - 30-90 days = constrained, focus on AR/AP optimization
   803→   - >90 days = healthy, can invest in growth
   804→
   805→2. **Cash Conversion Cycle** = DSO + DIO - DPO
   806→   - Goal: Minimize this number
   807→   - Best-in-class: Negative CCC (Dell model—get paid before you pay suppliers)
   808→   - If >90 days = working capital problem, prioritize before growth initiatives
   809→
   810→**Why this matters first:** Companies go bankrupt from cash problems, not P&L problems. A profitable company with poor cash management can fail.
   811→
   812→### Tier 2: Unit Economics (If Cash is Healthy)
   813→3. **CAC Payback Period** = CAC ÷ (ARPU × Gross Margin)
   814→   - SaaS benchmark: <12 months
   815→   - If >18 months = unsustainable growth, fix go-to-market efficiency
   816→   - If <6 months = underspending on growth, could accelerate
   817→
   818→4. **LTV:CAC Ratio** = (ARPU × Avg Lifetime × Gross Margin) ÷ CAC
   819→   - Healthy: 3:1 or better
   820→   - If <3:1 = retention problem or acquisition too expensive
   821→   - If >5:1 = potentially underspending on sales/marketing
   822→
   823→**Why this is second:** Growth doesn't matter if unit economics are broken. Fix this before scaling.
   824→
   825→### Tier 3: Growth Efficiency (If Units Work)
   826→5. **Rule of 40** = Revenue Growth % + FCF Margin %
   827→   - Benchmark: >40% = healthy SaaS business
   828→   - If <40% = growing too fast at expense of profitability, or too profitable but not growing
   829→   - Tradeoff: Early stage should prioritize growth (60% growth + -20% margin = 40)
   830→   - Mature: Should prioritize margin (20% growth + 25% margin = 45)
   831→
   832→**Why this is third:** Only relevant if you have cash and healthy units.
   833→
   834→## Executive Decision Points
   835→
   836→**Scenario: Revenue growth is slowing**
   837→
   838→CFO decision tree:
   839→1. Check CAC Payback—is it increasing?
   840→   - Yes → GTM efficiency problem, not market problem
   841→   - No → Could be market saturation, need new segments
   842→
   843→2. Check Net Revenue Retention (NRR)
   844→   - NRR <100% → Churn problem, fix product/CS before spending on acquisition
   845→   - NRR >110% → Healthy, can invest in new customer acquisition
   846→
   847→3. Check Sales Efficiency (New ARR ÷ Sales & Marketing Spend)
   848→   - Decreasing → Diminishing returns, current channels tapped out
   849→   - Stable → Can scale spend proportionally
   850→   - Increasing → Pour gas on fire, increase S&M budget
   851→
   852→**This is what separates good CFOs from great ones:** They diagnose ROOT CAUSE before reacting. Slowing growth could mean 5 different things—the metrics tell you which.
   853→
   854→## Red Flags to Watch For
   855→
   856→When user asks for financial analysis, check for:
   857→- ❌ "We need to cut costs" → WHY? Cash problem or profitability problem? Different solutions.
   858→- ❌ "Revenue is down" → Compared to what? Plan? Last year? Last quarter? Seasonal?
   859→- ❌ "Should we raise prices?" → What's your NRR? If <100%, pricing isn't the problem.
   860→
   861→## References
   862→
   863→- `references/financial-modeling.md` - DCF, LBO, M&A models
   864→- `references/ratio-analysis.md` - Deep dive on 50+ financial ratios
   865→- `references/investor-relations.md` - Board presentation frameworks
   866→```
   867→
   868→**Why this is expert-level:**
   869→- **Prioritization**: Not all metrics matter equally—shows what to check first
   870→- **Decision frameworks**: Explicit if/then logic executives actually use
   871→- **Thresholds**: Specific numbers (30 days cash, 3:1 LTV:CAC) not vague "good/bad"
   872→- **Tradeoffs**: Explains when to prioritize growth vs. profitability
   873→- **Root cause diagnosis**: Shows how to interpret metrics to find real problems
   874→- **Domain vocabulary**: CAC, NRR, Rule of 40 = terms professionals use
   875→
   876→**Rule for Domain Expert Skills:**
   877→1. Include executive decision frameworks (if X, then Y)
   878→2. Specify numeric thresholds and benchmarks (not "good margin"—say ">40%")
   879→3. Show prioritization logic (what to check first, second, third)
   880→4. Explain tradeoffs (when to choose A vs. B)
   881→5. Use actual professional terminology
   882→6. Include diagnosis patterns (how to find root causes)
   883→
   884→---
   885→
   886→#### Issue 2: Missing Industry Context
   887→
   888→**What happens:** Skill gives advice that's technically correct but doesn't account for industry-specific constraints
   889→**Why it matters:** What works in tech doesn't work in healthcare; what works in retail doesn't work in manufacturing
   890→
   891→**Example - Missing Context:**
   892→```markdown
   893→# Fortune 50 Operations Expert
   894→
   895→## Inventory Management
   896→
   897→Optimal inventory strategy:
   898→- Minimize inventory holding costs
   899→- Use just-in-time (JIT) delivery
   900→- Target <30 days inventory on hand
   901→- Reduce warehouse footprint
   902→```
   903→
   904→**This is wrong because:**
   905→- JIT works for automotive, fails for retail (need stock for seasonal demand)
   906→- Tech companies have different inventory than pharmaceutical (perishability)
   907→- Ignores industry-specific constraints (regulatory, lead times, volatility)
   908→
   909→**Correct Approach:**
   910→```markdown
   911→# Fortune 50 Operations Expert
   912→
   913→## Inventory Strategy: Industry-Specific Frameworks
   914→
   915→### Before Recommending Strategy, Classify Industry
   916→
   917→**Ask user:** What industry/business model?
   918→
   919→Then apply appropriate framework:
   920→
   921→#### Tech Hardware / Automotive (JIT Model)
   922→**When to use:**
   923→- Predictable demand
   924→- Reliable supplier base
   925→- Short product lifecycles (components depreciate fast)
   926→
   927→**Strategy:**
   928→- Target: <30 days inventory
   929→- Just-in-time delivery
   930→- Vendor-managed inventory (VMI)
   931→- Focus: Minimize holding costs
   932→
   933→**Why:** iPhone component costs drop 20% per quarter—holding inventory = losing money
   934→
   935→---
   936→
   937→#### Retail / Consumer Goods (Seasonal Buffering)
   938→**When to use:**
   939→- Seasonal demand spikes (holidays)
   940→- Long supplier lead times (overseas manufacturing)
   941→- Price-sensitive customers (can't pass on rush shipping)
   942→
   943→**Strategy:**
   944→- Target: 60-90 days inventory (build buffer before peak season)
   945→- ABC analysis: Stock A-items deep, C-items shallow
   946→- Pre-season orders with suppliers
   947→- Focus: Product availability during peak
   948→
   949→**Why:** Stockout during holiday season = lost sale forever (customer buys from competitor)
   950→
   951→---
   952→
   953→#### Pharmaceutical / Medical (Regulatory-Driven)
   954→**When to use:**
   955→- Regulatory expiration dates
   956→- Critical availability requirements (can't stockout)
   957→- Long validation cycles for new suppliers
   958→
   959→**Strategy:**
   960→- Target: 120+ days inventory (account for expiration)
   961→- Dual sourcing for critical items
   962→- FEFO (First Expire, First Out)
   963→- Focus: Compliance + availability
   964→
   965→**Why:** Switching suppliers requires FDA revalidation (6-12 months)—can't just find new supplier
   966→
   967→---
   968→
   969→## Decision Framework
   970→
   971→**Q: User says "We have too much inventory"**
   972→
   973→Before answering, determine:
   974→1. What industry? (Determines "too much" threshold)
   975→2. Inventory turnover ratio? (COGS ÷ Avg Inventory)
   976→   - Tech: >10x is good
   977→   - Retail: >5x is good
   978→   - Manufacturing: >3x is good
   979→3. Obsolescence risk? (Short lifecycle = reduce inventory)
   980→4. Stockout cost? (Critical availability = keep more inventory)
   981→
   982→Then recommend strategy based on their specific context.
   983→
   984→## References
   985→
   986→- `references/supply-chain-optimization.md` - Industry-specific SCM strategies
   987→- `references/inventory-models.md` - EOQ, JIT, VMI, ABC analysis deep dives
   988→```
   989→
   990→**Why this works:**
   991→- **Context-aware**: Asks industry before recommending strategy
   992→- **Industry frameworks**: Different advice for tech vs. retail vs. pharma
   993→- **Tradeoffs explicit**: Explains WHY strategies differ (lifecycle, regulation, seasonality)
   994→- **Decision support**: Helps determine which model applies
   995→
   996→---
   997→
   998→### Generic Code Skills (generic-*)
   999→
  1000→#### Issue 1: Checklist Without Reasoning
  1001→
  1002→**What happens:** Skill is just a list of things to check, no guidance on prioritization or judgment
  1003→**Why it's wrong:** Claude doesn't know WHEN to enforce rules strictly vs. when to be flexible
  1004→
  1005→**Example - Pure Checklist:**
  1006→```markdown
  1007→# Generic Code Reviewer
  1008→
  1009→## Review Checklist
  1010→
  1011→- [ ] No TypeScript `any` types
  1012→- [ ] All functions <50 lines
  1013→- [ ] No console.log statements
  1014→- [ ] All variables use const/let
  1015→- [ ] No hardcoded strings
  1016→- [ ] All errors handled
  1017→- [ ] WCAG AA compliance
  1018→- [ ] Tests included
  1019→```
  1020→
  1021→**Problems:**
  1022→- No prioritization (are all these equal?)
  1023→- No context (50 lines always? or guideline?)
  1024→- No judgment (when is breaking a rule OK?)
  1025→
  1026→**Correct Approach:**
  1027→```markdown
  1028→# Generic Code Reviewer
  1029→
  1030→## Review Philosophy: Surgical Simplicity
  1031→
  1032→Not all rules are equal. Prioritize:
  1033→1. **Correctness** - Does it work?
  1034→2. **Safety** - Type safety, security, error handling
  1035→3. **Maintainability** - Can next dev understand it?
  1036→4. **Performance** - Only if user-facing impact
  1037→
  1038→## P0 Issues (Block Merge)
  1039→
  1040→### Type Safety
  1041→**Rule:** No `any` types
  1042→**Why:** `any` defeats TypeScript's purpose
  1043→**Exception:** External library without types → use `unknown` and validate at runtime
  1044→**How to flag:** "Found `any` type in X. Use `SomeType` instead, or `unknown` if truly dynamic."
  1045→
  1046→### Security
  1047→**Rule:** No `innerHTML`, no exposed secrets, sanitize inputs
  1048→**Why:** Prevents XSS, credential leaks, injection
  1049→**Exception:** NEVER. Security rules have no exceptions.
  1050→**How to flag:** "BLOCKING: Found `innerHTML` at line X. Use `textContent` or DOMPurify."
  1051→
  1052→### Error Handling
  1053→**Rule:** Async functions must have try/catch or `.catch()`
  1054→**Why:** Unhandled promise rejections crash apps
  1055→**Exception:** If error is handled by framework (React Error Boundary, Next.js error.tsx)
  1056→**How to flag:** "Async function X doesn't handle errors. Add try/catch or document why framework handles this."
  1057→
  1058→## P1 Issues (Should Fix)
  1059→
  1060→### Function Size
  1061→**Guideline:** <50 lines preferred
  1062→**Why:** Easier to test, understand, reuse
  1063→**Exception:** Complex algorithms where splitting hurts readability (mark with comment explaining)
  1064→**How to flag:** "Function X is 87 lines. Consider extracting Y and Z into separate functions."
  1065→
  1066→### Code Duplication
  1067→**Guideline:** Extract after 3rd repetition (DRY)
  1068→**Why:** Single source of truth
  1069→**Exception:** Similar-looking code that serves different purposes (don't force abstraction)
  1070→**How to flag:** "Found repeated pattern in X, Y, Z. Consider creating shared utility `doThing()`."
  1071→
  1072→## P2 Issues (Nice to Have)
  1073→
  1074→### Performance
  1075→**Guideline:** No obvious bottlenecks
  1076→**Why:** User experience
  1077→**When to mention:** If you see N+1 queries, unnecessary re-renders, or blocking operations on hot paths
  1078→**When to skip:** Micro-optimizations, code that runs once on load
  1079→**How to flag:** "This re-renders on every keystroke. Consider debouncing or useMemo."
  1080→
  1081→## Review Output Format
  1082→
  1083→Only report issues found (don't list empty categories):
  1084→
  1085→**Blocking Issues:**
  1086→- [Only if found]
  1087→
  1088→**Should Fix:**
  1089→- [Only if found]
  1090→
  1091→**Consider:**
  1092→- [Only if found]
  1093→
  1094→**If no issues:** "Code looks good. Follows type safety and security standards."
  1095→
  1096→## Judgment Calls
  1097→
  1098→**When user asks "Is this OK?"**
  1099→
  1100→Consider:
  1101→- Is this production code or prototype? (Stricter for production)
  1102→- Is this hot path or one-time setup? (Performance matters more on hot path)
  1103→- Is this public API or internal helper? (More rigor for public APIs)
  1104→
  1105→Adjust expectations accordingly.
  1106→```
  1107→
  1108→**Why this works:**
  1109→- **Prioritization**: P0/P1/P2 hierarchy prevents overwhelming with minor issues
  1110→- **Reasoning**: Explains WHY each rule matters
  1111→- **Exceptions**: Shows when rules can flex (prevents rigid enforcement)
  1112→- **Judgment**: Guides context-aware decisions
  1113→- **Selective output**: Only reports actual issues, not full checklist
  1114→
  1115→---
  1116→
  1117→#### Issue 2: Tech Stack Assumptions
  1118→
  1119→**What happens:** Skill assumes React/TypeScript when user might be using Vue/Python
  1120→**Why it's wrong:** "Generic" skills should adapt to user's stack, not impose assumptions
  1121→
  1122→**Example - Assumed Stack:**
  1123→```markdown
  1124→# Generic Feature Developer
  1125→
  1126→## Component Structure
  1127→
  1128→Create React components following this pattern:
  1129→
  1130→```typescript
  1131→interface Props {
  1132→  title: string;
  1133→}
  1134→
  1135→export function Component({ title }: Props) {
  1136→  return <div>{title}</div>;
  1137→}
  1138→```
  1139→
  1140→Use hooks for state management...
  1141→```
  1142→
  1143→**Problem:** User might be building:
  1144→- Vue app (use SFCs, not React components)
  1145→- Python backend (no TypeScript)
  1146→- Static HTML site (no components)
  1147→
  1148→**Correct Approach:**
  1149→```markdown
  1150→# Generic Feature Developer
  1151→
  1152→## Architecture Guidance (Stack-Agnostic)
  1153→
  1154→### Step 1: Identify Tech Stack
  1155→
  1156→**Before suggesting architecture, determine:**
  1157→- What framework/language? (React, Vue, Python, static HTML, etc.)
  1158→- What patterns does this project already use?
  1159→
  1160→**Check existing code:**
  1161→```bash
  1162→# Look for package.json (JavaScript project)
  1163→# Look for requirements.txt (Python project)
  1164→# Look for *.vue files (Vue project)
  1165→# Look for tsx/jsx (React project)
  1166→```
  1167→
  1168→### Step 2: Apply Stack-Specific Patterns
  1169→
  1170→#### For React/TypeScript Projects
  1171→- Use functional components + hooks
  1172→- Props interface required
  1173→- Prefer composition over inheritance
  1174→
  1175→```typescript
  1176→interface Props { title: string }
  1177→export function Component({ title }: Props) {
  1178→  return <div>{title}</div>
  1179→}
  1180→```
  1181→
  1182→---
  1183→
  1184→#### For Vue Projects
  1185→- Use Single File Components (SFC)
  1186→- Composition API preferred over Options API
  1187→- TypeScript with defineProps
  1188→
  1189→```vue
  1190→<script setup lang="ts">
  1191→defineProps<{ title: string }>()
  1192→</script>
  1193→
  1194→<template>
  1195→  <div>{{ title }}</div>
  1196→</template>
  1197→```
  1198→
  1199→---
  1200→
  1201→#### For Python Projects
  1202→- Type hints on all functions
  1203→- Pydantic for validation
  1204→- Dependency injection for services
  1205→
  1206→```python
  1207→from pydantic import BaseModel
  1208→
  1209→class UserInput(BaseModel):
  1210→    title: str
  1211→
  1212→def process(data: UserInput) -> dict:
  1213→    return {"result": data.title}
  1214→```
  1215→
  1216→---
  1217→
  1218→#### For Static HTML Sites
  1219→- No build step, pure HTML/CSS/JS
  1220→- ES6+ syntax OK (modern browsers)
  1221→- Event delegation for performance
  1222→
  1223→```html
  1224→<div id="app"></div>
  1225→<script>
  1226→  const app = document.getElementById('app');
  1227→  app.textContent = 'Hello';
  1228→</script>
  1229→```
  1230→
  1231→### Step 3: Follow Project Conventions
  1232→
  1233→**Before suggesting pattern, check:**
  1234→- How do existing files structure code?
  1235→- What naming conventions are used?
  1236→- Are there linting rules to follow?
  1237→
  1238→**Match existing patterns > impose new ones**
  1239→
  1240→## When Stack is Unclear
  1241→
  1242→If you can't determine stack from context:
  1243→
  1244→**Ask:**
  1245→"I see this could be [React/Vue/Python/etc.]. What framework are you using? This will help me suggest the right architecture."
  1246→
  1247→**Never assume** and generate code in wrong framework.
  1248→```
  1249→
  1250→**Why this works:**
  1251→- **Detection first**: Checks project before suggesting patterns
  1252→- **Multi-stack support**: Covers common stacks
  1253→- **Respects existing**: Follows project conventions over skill preferences
  1254→- **Fails safely**: Asks if unclear rather than guessing wrong
  1255→
  1256→---
  1257→
  1258→### Utility Skills (Workflow/Tool Skills)
  1259→
  1260→#### Issue: Tool Description Without Usage Workflow
  1261→
  1262→**What happens:** Skill explains what it CAN do but not HOW/WHEN to use it
  1263→**Why it's wrong:** User doesn't know when to invoke skill or what steps to follow
  1264→
  1265→**Example - Tool Description Only:**
  1266→```markdown
  1267→# Create Slash Command
  1268→
  1269→This skill helps you create custom slash commands for Claude Code.
  1270→
  1271→Slash commands are shortcuts that execute prompts. They can include:
  1272→- Static prompts
  1273→- Dynamic arguments
  1274→- YAML frontmatter
  1275→- Conditional logic
  1276→
  1277→The skill generates command files in `.claude/commands/` directory.
  1278→```
  1279→
  1280→**Problem:**
  1281→- No workflow (what are the steps?)
  1282→- No decision guidance (when to use vs. not use?)
  1283→- No examples (what does good output look like?)
  1284→
  1285→**Correct Approach:**
  1286→```markdown
  1287→# Create Slash Command
  1288→
  1289→## When to Use This Skill
  1290→
  1291→**Create a slash command when:**
  1292→- User repeats same multi-step task frequently
  1293→- Task needs context (arguments, dynamic inputs)
  1294→- Workflow is established and reusable
  1295→
  1296→**Don't create a slash command when:**
  1297→- Task is one-off or rarely used (just do it directly)
  1298→- Workflow is still being figured out (too early to codify)
  1299→- Task is simple enough to remember without command
  1300→
  1301→## Workflow: Creating a Slash Command
  1302→
  1303→### Step 1: Gather Requirements
  1304→
  1305→**Ask user (if not provided):**
  1306→1. What should the command be called? (e.g., `/deploy`, `/test-feature`)
  1307→2. What arguments does it need? (e.g., environment name, feature flag)
  1308→3. What's the step-by-step process to automate?
  1309→4. Are there any conditional steps? (e.g., skip tests if flag set)
  1310→
  1311→### Step 2: Design Command Structure
  1312→
  1313→**Basic command anatomy:**
  1314→```markdown
  1315→---
  1316→name: command-name
  1317→description: What this command does (one line)
  1318→arguments:
  1319→  - name: arg1
  1320→    description: What this argument is for
  1321→    required: true
  1322→---
  1323→
  1324→# Command: {name}
  1325→
  1326→## What This Does
  1327→[Explain the workflow]
  1328→
  1329→## Steps
  1330→1. First step
  1331→2. Second step
  1332→3. Third step
  1333→
  1334→## Example Usage
  1335→/command-name value1
  1336→```
  1337→
  1338→### Step 3: Write Command File
  1339→
  1340→**File location:** `.claude/commands/{name}.md`
  1341→
  1342→**Example - Deploy Command:**
  1343→```markdown
  1344→---
  1345→name: deploy
  1346→description: Deploy application to specified environment
  1347→arguments:
  1348→  - name: environment
  1349→    description: Target environment (staging, production)
  1350→    required: true
  1351→  - name: skip-tests
  1352→    description: Skip test suite (use for hotfixes)
  1353→    required: false
  1354→---
  1355→
  1356→# Command: Deploy to {environment}
  1357→
  1358→## Pre-Deployment Checks
  1359→
  1360→1. Verify environment is valid:
  1361→   - If not "staging" or "production", error and exit
  1362→
  1363→2. Check git status:
  1364→   - If uncommitted changes, warn user
  1365→
  1366→3. Run tests (unless skip-tests flag set):
  1367→   ```bash
  1368→   npm run test
  1369→   ```
  1370→   - If tests fail, stop deployment
  1371→
  1372→## Deployment Steps
  1373→
  1374→4. Build for {environment}:
  1375→   ```bash
  1376→   npm run build:{environment}
  1377→   ```
  1378→
  1379→5. Deploy to {environment}:
  1380→   ```bash
  1381→   ./scripts/deploy.sh {environment}
  1382→   ```
  1383→
  1384→6. Verify deployment:
  1385→   ```bash
  1386→   curl https://{environment}.example.com/health
  1387→   ```
  1388→
  1389→7. Notify user of success/failure
  1390→
  1391→## Example Usage
  1392→
  1393→Deploy to staging:
  1394→```
  1395→/deploy staging
  1396→```
  1397→
  1398→Deploy to production (skip tests for hotfix):
  1399→```
  1400→/deploy production --skip-tests
  1401→```
  1402→```
  1403→
  1404→### Step 4: Test Command
  1405→
  1406→**After creating, verify:**
  1407→1. Run `/deploy staging` to test
  1408→2. Check that arguments are captured correctly
  1409→3. Verify conditional logic works (try `--skip-tests`)
  1410→
  1411→### Step 5: Document for User
  1412→
  1413→**Tell user:**
  1414→"Created `/deploy` command at `.claude/commands/deploy.md`
  1415→
  1416→**Usage:**
  1417→- Deploy to staging: `/deploy staging`
  1418→- Deploy to production: `/deploy production`
  1419→- Skip tests (hotfix): `/deploy production --skip-tests`
  1420→
  1421→**Next steps:**
  1422→- Test with `/deploy staging` to verify workflow
  1423→- Adjust steps if needed"
  1424→
  1425→## Quality Checklist
  1426→
  1427→Before delivering slash command, verify:
  1428→- [ ] Name is clear and follows existing command naming
  1429→- [ ] Description is one line, concise
  1430→- [ ] Arguments are documented with required/optional
  1431→- [ ] Steps are numbered and sequential
  1432→- [ ] Examples show actual usage
  1433→- [ ] Conditional logic is explicit (if X, then Y)
  1434→
  1435→## Common Mistakes to Avoid
  1436→
  1437→**❌ Vague steps:**
  1438→```
  1439→1. Check environment
  1440→2. Deploy code
  1441→```
  1442→
  1443→**✅ Specific steps:**
  1444→```
  1445→1. Verify environment argument is "staging" or "production"
  1446→   - If neither, show error: "Invalid environment. Use 'staging' or 'production'"
  1447→   - Exit if invalid
  1448→2. Run deployment script:
  1449→   ```bash
  1450→   ./scripts/deploy.sh {environment}
  1451→   ```
  1452→   - Capture output
  1453→   - If exit code ≠ 0, report failure
  1454→```
  1455→
  1456→## References
  1457→
  1458→- `.claude/commands/` - Existing commands to reference
  1459→- Claude Code docs on slash command syntax
  1460→```
  1461→
  1462→**Why this works:**
  1463→- **Decision guidance**: When to use vs. not use
  1464→- **Step-by-step workflow**: Gather requirements → Design → Write → Test → Document
  1465→- **Concrete examples**: Shows complete command file, not just description
  1466→- **Quality checks**: Prevents common mistakes
  1467→- **Usage examples**: Shows user what they'll get
  1468→
  1469→**Rule for Utility Skills:**
  1470→1. Start with "When to use" vs. "When not to use"
  1471→2. Provide numbered workflow (Step 1, Step 2, etc.)
  1472→3. Include complete examples of outputs
  1473→4. Add quality checklist
  1474→5. Show common mistakes to avoid
  1475→
  1476→---
  1477→
  1478→## Decision Risks
  1479→
  1480→### Risk: `generic-*` Naming Convention
  1481→
  1482→**Risk Level:** LOW
  1483→**Concern:** "Generic" might imply low quality or basic functionality
  1484→**Counter-evidence:** Naming matches existing project structure; describes applicability (works across stacks) not quality level
  1485→**Mitigation:** Ensure skill descriptions emphasize "works with any stack" not "basic/simple"
  1486→
  1487→**Example:**
  1488→- ❌ "Generic code review for simple projects"
  1489→- ✅ "Code review for any tech stack - adapts to React, Vue, Python, static sites, etc."
  1490→
  1491→---
  1492→
  1493→### Risk: SKILL.md + references/ Structure
  1494→
  1495→**Risk Level:** MEDIUM
  1496→**Concern:** Claude might not read reference files when needed, defaulting to incomplete guidance from SKILL.md
  1497→**Real Issue:** If SKILL.md doesn't explicitly cue Claude to check references/ at decision points, deep content might be ignored
  1498→
  1499→**Mitigation Strategies:**
  1500→
  1501→#### 1. Explicit Signposting in SKILL.md
  1502→
  1503→**Instead of:**
  1504→```markdown
  1505→## Brand Strategy
  1506→
  1507→When developing brand strategy, consider positioning, architecture, and equity.
  1508→```
  1509→
  1510→**Use:**
  1511→```markdown
  1512→## Brand Strategy Decision Point
  1513→
  1514→**For quick brand decisions:** Use framework below
  1515→**For comprehensive brand strategy:** READ `references/brand-strategy.md` for portfolio architecture, equity measurement, and master brand vs. sub-brand decisions
  1516→
  1517→### Quick Framework (Use for Simple Cases)
  1518→[Condensed guidance]
  1519→
  1520→### When to Go Deep
  1521→If user asks about:
  1522→- Multi-product brand architecture → READ references/brand-strategy.md
  1523→- Brand equity measurement → READ references/brand-strategy.md
  1524→- Master brand vs. sub-brand decisions → READ references/brand-strategy.md
  1525→```
  1526→
  1527→#### 2. Decision Trees Point to References
  1528→
  1529→```markdown
  1530→## Marketing Strategy: Decision Tree
  1531→
  1532→**Question 1:** Is this a product launch or ongoing campaign?
  1533→- Product launch → READ references/gtm-playbook.md (comprehensive launch framework)
  1534→- Ongoing campaign → Continue to Question 2
  1535→
  1536→**Question 2:** What's the primary goal?
  1537→- Demand generation → Use quick framework below
  1538→- Brand awareness → READ references/brand-strategy.md
  1539→```
  1540→
  1541→#### 3. Reference Inventory in SKILL.md
  1542→
  1543→```markdown
  1544→# Fortune 50 Marketing Expert
  1545→
  1546→## Reference Library
  1547→
  1548→When user needs deep guidance, point them to:
  1549→- `references/brand-strategy.md` - Portfolio architecture, equity, positioning
  1550→- `references/gtm-playbook.md` - Product launch, go-to-market strategy
  1551→- `references/campaign-playbook.md` - Campaign planning, media mix
  1552→- `references/analytics-frameworks.md` - Attribution, CAC, LTV modeling
  1553→
  1554→**How to use:** When conversation requires depth beyond quick frameworks below, READ relevant reference and apply to user's specific situation.
  1555→```
  1556→
  1557→#### 4. Size Threshold Guidance
  1558→
  1559→**SKILL.md should be:**
  1560→- ~5-10KB max
  1561→- Core decision frameworks
  1562→- Activation logic
  1563→- Signposts to references
  1564→
  1565→**references/ should contain:**
  1566→- Deep dives (10-20KB each)
  1567→- Comprehensive frameworks
  1568→- Detailed examples
  1569→- Industry-specific adaptations
  1570→
  1571→**Test:** If SKILL.md alone can handle 80% of queries effectively, structure is correct. If users frequently need references, consider moving more key content into SKILL.md.
  1572→
  1573→---
  1574→
  1575→### Risk: Inflating Thin Skills with Padding
  1576→
  1577→**Risk Level:** HIGH
  1578→**Concern:** Claude might add generic filler to make thin skills appear more substantial
  1579→**Why it's dangerous:** Bloated skills waste tokens, reduce activation reliability, and don't actually improve quality
  1580→
  1581→**Example of Padding:**
  1582→```markdown
  1583→# UX Designer
  1584→
  1585→## Introduction
  1586→User experience design is a critical discipline that shapes how users interact with products. Good UX design creates intuitive, accessible, and delightful experiences. Throughout the years, UX has evolved from basic usability testing to comprehensive design thinking methodologies that encompass user research, interaction design, visual design, and usability evaluation.
  1587→
  1588→## History of UX
  1589→[3000 words of UX history that doesn't help Claude do the job]
  1590→
  1591→## Importance of UX
  1592→[2000 words explaining why UX matters]
  1593→
  1594→## Core Principles
  1595→[Generic principles found in any UX textbook]
  1596→```
  1597→
  1598→**This is padding because:**
  1599→- History doesn't change how Claude should approach UX tasks
  1600→- "Importance" doesn't provide actionable guidance
  1601→- Generic principles without application context waste tokens
  1602→
  1603→**Correct Approach for Thin Skills:**
  1604→
  1605→If a skill is genuinely thin (limited scope), **keep it thin**:
  1606→
  1607→```markdown
  1608→# UX Quick Critique
  1609→
  1610→## When to Use
  1611→When user asks for fast UX feedback on specific UI elements or flows (not comprehensive UX strategy).
  1612→
  1613→## Critique Framework
  1614→
  1615→Evaluate against:
  1616→
  1617→1. **Clarity**: Can user understand what this does in <3 seconds?
  1618→   - If no: What's confusing? Suggest clearer labels/hierarchy
  1619→
  1620→2. **Accessibility**: Can everyone use this?
  1621→   - Check: Color contrast, keyboard navigation, screen reader support
  1622→   - Flag issues, suggest fixes
  1623→
  1624→3. **Efficiency**: Minimum steps to complete task?
  1625→   - Count clicks/taps
  1626→   - If >3 steps for simple task: Suggest streamlining
  1627→
  1628→4. **Consistency**: Matches existing patterns in this product?
  1629→   - If deviates: Is there a good reason? If not, align with existing
  1630→
  1631→## Output Format
  1632→
  1633→**Quick Wins:**
  1634→- [Easy improvements]
  1635→
  1636→**Accessibility Issues:**
  1637→- [WCAG AA violations]
  1638→
  1639→**Suggested Changes:**
  1640→- [Specific recommendations]
  1641→
  1642→## When to Use Different Skill
  1643→
  1644→If user needs:
  1645→- Complete UX strategy → Use `generic-ux-designer` skill
  1646→- Design system → Use `generic-design-system` skill
  1647→- User research plan → Use `generic-ux-designer` skill
  1648→```
  1649→
  1650→**Why this is correct:**
  1651→- Focused scope (quick critiques only)
  1652→- Actionable framework (4 criteria with specific checks)
  1653→- Acknowledges limitations (points to other skills for comprehensive work)
  1654→- No padding (every sentence serves a purpose)
  1655→
  1656→**Rule:**
  1657→- 5KB of focused, actionable content > 15KB with padding
  1658→- If skill is legitimately narrow scope, keep it narrow
  1659→- Add references/ for depth, not padding in SKILL.md
  1660→- Every paragraph must answer: "How does this help Claude do the job better?"
  1661→
  1662→**Quality test:** Can you remove any section without reducing Claude's ability to perform the task? If yes, remove it.
  1663→
  1664→---
  1665→
  1666→## Critical Warnings
  1667→
  1668→### 1. Don't Inflate Thin Skills with Padding
  1669→
  1670→**Why:** Bloat wastes tokens, reduces activation reliability, and doesn't improve quality
  1671→**How to avoid:** Every sentence must answer "How does this help Claude perform the task?"
  1672→**Test:** If you can remove a section without reducing task performance, remove it
  1673→**Better approach:** Keep focused skills focused; add references/ for depth, not padding
  1674→
  1675→---
  1676→
  1677→### 2. Don't Lose Backward Compatibility
  1678→
  1679→**Why:** Existing users rely on current activation patterns
  1680→**How to avoid:**
  1681→- Keep existing description trigger phrases
  1682→- Add new triggers, don't replace old ones
  1683→- Test that old queries still activate skill
  1684→**Example:**
  1685→- Old: "Use for marketing strategy"
  1686→- New: "Use for marketing strategy, campaigns, brand development, product launches..." (adds triggers, keeps old)
  1687→
  1688→---
  1689→
  1690→### 3. Don't Add Theory Without Application
  1691→
  1692→**Why:** LLMs default to explaining concepts rather than executing tasks
  1693→**How to avoid:**
  1694→- Every framework must include step-by-step usage
  1695→- Every concept must have "when to use" + "how to apply"
  1696→- Provide examples showing application, not just explanation
  1697→**Test:** After reading skill, can Claude execute the task? Or just explain it?
  1698→
  1699→---
  1700→
  1701→### 4. Don't Forget Activation Testing
  1702→
  1703→**Why:** Skills that don't trigger on natural language are useless
  1704→**How to avoid:**
  1705→- List 10 ways users might describe this task
  1706→- Ensure description field includes those phrases
  1707→- Test: Would skill activate if user said "Our code is messy"?
  1708→**Tool:** After writing description, generate test queries and verify activation
  1709→
  1710→---
  1711→
  1712→### 5. Don't Exceed ~10 Simultaneous Instructions
  1713→
  1714→**Why:** Research proves LLM performance degrades 19% when overloaded; Claude follows 10+ instructions only 44-58% of the time
  1715→**How to avoid:**
  1716→- Use tiered priorities (P0/P1/P2) instead of flat lists
  1717→- Break complex skills into decision trees
  1718→- Group related instructions into single directive
  1719→**Test:** Count explicit instructions in skill; if >10, refactor into hierarchy
  1720→
  1721→**Sources:**
  1722→- [OpenReview - Curse of Instructions](https://openreview.net/forum?id=R6q67CDBCH)
  1723→- [ArXiv - LLM Instruction Following Limits](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.11538)
  1724→
  1725→---
  1726→
  1727→### 6. Under-Specification Causes Regression
  1728→
  1729→**Why:** Vague requirements are 2x more likely to break across model updates, with accuracy drops >20%
  1730→**How to avoid:**
  1731→- Specify audience, format, and constraints explicitly
  1732→- Don't rely on Claude to "figure out" what you mean
  1733→- Include success criteria and output format
  1734→**Test:** Could a different model/version interpret this skill differently? If yes, add specificity
  1735→
  1736→**Source:** [ArXiv - Underspecification in LLM Prompts](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.13360v1)
  1737→
  1738→---
  1739→
  1740→### 7. Always Include Self-Critique for Technical Output
  1741→
  1742→**Why:** Research shows prompting LLM to verify its own work significantly reduces errors
  1743→**How to avoid:** Any skill generating code, SQL, configs, or formulas must include mandatory verification step
  1744→**Pattern:**
  1745→```markdown
  1746→## Step N: Self-Critique (REQUIRED)
  1747→
  1748→Before presenting output, check:
  1749→- [ ] Correctness: Does this actually work?
  1750→- [ ] Edge cases: What could break this?
  1751→- [ ] Performance: Any obvious bottlenecks?
  1752→
  1753→If any issues found, revise and re-check.
  1754→```
  1755→
  1756→**Source:** [Medium - Prompt Engineering Mistakes](https://medium.com/@kaushalsinh73/5-prompt-engineering-mistakes-that-cost-me-accuracy-and-how-to-fix-them-83204a638769)
  1757→
  1758→---
  1759→
  1760→## Token Efficiency Pitfalls
  1761→
  1762→### Common Token Waste Patterns
  1763→
  1764→1. **Repeating system context in every reference file**
  1765→   - ❌ Every references/*.md starts with "You are a Fortune 50 expert..."
  1766→   - ✅ Context in SKILL.md once; references assume it
  1767→
  1768→2. **Verbose explanations instead of direct instructions**
  1769→   - ❌ "When considering the various factors that might influence..."
  1770→   - ✅ "Consider these factors:"
  1771→
  1772→3. **Example bloat (too many examples)**
  1773→   - ❌ 10 examples of the same pattern
  1774→   - ✅ 2-3 carefully selected examples showing variation
  1775→
  1776→4. **Defensive language and caveats**
  1777→   - ❌ "This might not work in all cases, but generally speaking, you could potentially..."
  1778→   - ✅ "Use this pattern for X. Exception: Y."
  1779→
  1780→### Token Optimization Strategies
  1781→
  1782→**Concise instruction patterns:**
  1783→
  1784→❌ Verbose:
  1785→```markdown
  1786→I would recommend that you first take a moment to carefully consider whether or not the user's request is something that would benefit from a comprehensive analysis or if perhaps a quicker, more streamlined response might be more appropriate given the context.
  1787→```
  1788→
  1789→✅ Concise:
  1790→```markdown
  1791→Determine: Does user need comprehensive analysis or quick answer?
  1792→- Comprehensive: READ references/deep-dive.md
  1793→- Quick: Use framework below
  1794→```
  1795→
  1796→**Savings:** 27 tokens → 15 tokens (44% reduction)
  1797→
  1798→---
  1799→
  1800→**Use structured formats over prose:**
  1801→
  1802→❌ Prose:
  1803→```markdown
  1804→The first thing you should do is check whether the code has any type safety issues. Look for places where the any type is being used, and also make sure that all the TypeScript strict mode checks are enabled in the tsconfig file.
  1805→```
  1806→
  1807→✅ Structured:
  1808→```markdown
  1809→1. Check type safety:
  1810→   - No `any` types → Use explicit types or `unknown`
  1811→   - Verify tsconfig.json: `"strict": true`
  1812→```
  1813→
  1814→**Savings:** 38 tokens → 18 tokens (53% reduction)
  1815→
  1816→---
  1817→
  1818→**Eliminate filler words:**
  1819→
  1820→| Filler | Direct |
  1821→|--------|---------|
  1822→| "In order to" | "To" |
  1823→| "At this point in time" | "Now" |
  1824→| "Due to the fact that" | "Because" |
  1825→| "It is important to note that" | [Delete entirely] |
  1826→| "One of the things that" | [Delete, get to point] |
  1827→
  1828→---
  1829→
  1830→**Reference file structure (token-efficient):**
  1831→
  1832→```markdown
  1833→# Brand Strategy Reference
  1834→
  1835→## Portfolio Architecture
  1836→
  1837→### Single Brand
  1838→**When:** One product, clear positioning
  1839→**Example:** Tesla (one brand, multiple products)
  1840→
  1841→### Master Brand + Sub-brands
  1842→**When:** Related products, shared equity
  1843→**Example:** Google (master) + Gmail, Maps (sub-brands)
  1844→
  1845→### House of Brands
  1846→**When:** Unrelated products, separate positioning
  1847→**Example:** P&G (Tide, Gillette, Pampers)
  1848→
  1849→## Decision Framework
  1850→
  1851→Revenue <$10M → Single brand (simplicity)
  1852→Revenue $10M-$100M → Master + sub-brands (leverage equity)
  1853→Revenue >$100M → Evaluate house of brands (optimize positioning)
  1854→```
  1855→
  1856→**No introductions, no conclusions, no "as mentioned above"—just frameworks and decisions.**
  1857→
  1858→---
  1859→
  1860→## Research Methodology
  1861→
  1862→This research synthesized findings from:
  1863→- Academic papers on LLM instruction-following limitations
  1864→- Claude official documentation on skill authoring
  1865→- Prompt engineering best practices (2025)
  1866→- Token optimization strategies
  1867→- Real-world skill activation failures
  1868→
  1869→**Key insight:** Most prompt/skill failures come from:
  1870→1. Overloading (>10 instructions)
  1871→2. Under-specification (forcing LLM to guess)
  1872→3. Missing examples (LLMs learn from examples, not just rules)
  1873→4. Poor activation (description doesn't match natural language)
  1874→5. Theory without action (explaining vs. executing)
  1875→
  1876→---
  1877→

  1879→
  1880→- [Lakera - Prompt Engineering Guide 2025](https://www.lakera.ai/blog/prompt-engineering-guide)
  1881→- [Latitude - Common LLM Prompt Engineering Challenges](https://latitude-blog.ghost.io/blog/common-llm-prompt-engineering-challenges-and-solutions/)
